sentences.text: Well, we made some examples of the most blatant ones.
They were tried and convicted.
And all of a sudden, we got supplements to [GERMAN] that people had filled up.
I made a mistake.
Instead of joining the party in 1938, as I said, I actually joined in 1932.
I made a slight mistake.

Of course, some people were so conscientious they said, I actually joined in-- in the end of 1937 instead of the end of 1938 or I neglected to say that I was in a-- a chess club-- and I joined a chess club or some innocuous organization that they didn't mention on the [GERMAN].
Of course, you know, we had to differentiate between somebody joining in 1938 and 1932.
In fact, we found somebody who joined in 1922, when Hitler first was one of the first members of the Nazi party.

And I found that out accidentally by a letter that we found that was signed by Himmler-- not Hitler, but Heinrich Himmler, one of his-- the head of the SS and of some [GERMAN], some old party members.
And he was a very respected person and they said, no, he was never a Nazi.
I couldn't-- I went to my assistant.
He knew all the people in town.
He says, he was an anti-Nazi if I ever saw one.
I said, but how is that possible?
So I'll call him into the office one-- one day.
And we started talking and I said, I have something here that disturbs me.
Is that the time when you actually joined the Nazi party, in 1938 or whenever it was?
So he said, yes.
But I said, weren't you a member of the Nazi party much earlier than that?
And all of a sudden, he turned white.
He said, yes.
I didn't even think it was relevant.
When-- after World War II, you know there was a lot of disillusionments here and a friend of mine talked me into coming to a meeting where Hitler spoke.
And he signed up as a supporter.
He felt-- I thought it was kind of stupid what he said.
And he said, can I put your name down?
And I said, oh, I'm not really into it.
Let me put my name down-- so-- put your name down.
So he went to one or two more meetings and he said after that, he said it was such nonsense he never even thought of attending another meeting.
So you have to be careful sometimes.

Sometimes it can-- you know, I was only 20 years old and I was not the most knowledgeable person in the world, but I learned very quickly and I had to learn very quickly and I had to make decisions which affected people's lives and I had to be conscious of it and it affected people's careers and reputations and so on.
And later on, we got rid of-- we had to screen all the prospective officials of the-- of the-- of the government.
The mayors have to be-- have to be-- there were no elections, they have to be appointed and officials have to-- had to be appointed.
And we also got-- it was terrible at that time-- denunciations where people denounced each other.
He did this and this and he did that and-- and-- and he shouldn't be appointed and he doesn't deserve to be appointed and somebody else should be.
And also, of course, nobody was a Nazi.
And that was the most terrible area where Nazism flourished earliest on.
And they-- they all say, oh, I was against Hitler.

But the reason they gave is not what he did, but that he started something that he didn't finish, the misery that he-- that the German people received when the cities were bombed and the people were killed and the soldiers didn't come back and the wounded were just starting to trickle back and the-- a lot of them was still in the Russian-- captured by the Russians, some of them were walking in horrible conditions and arriving.
And the first Jewish refugees came out of the concentration camps and some of them settled here in Neustadt.
And there was a very unpleasant and unhappy situation which took place.
Of course, we tried to help them as much as we could.
They were like skeletons.
We fed them, we gave them all the food that we could.
And we also put them into a winery where they would run the-- bottling wine and selling it and distributing it-- that belonged to a Nazi who had fled.
And they were very grateful that they could do that.
But their attitude was such they-- all of a sudden we got reports that they watered the wine and they did all kinds of things.
And when we confronted them, their rationale was after what the Germans did to us, we don't have any scruples treating them any way we can and taking advantage of them any way we can.
So we tried to instill them that they were supposed to be-- that we put them-- put them in there to act as models-- and as-- as role models more than anything else.
They could not understand that.
And we had to-- that was one of the saddest jobs that-- that I had.
Yes.
That was shortly before I left and I really don't know exactly what happened.
But I don't think they were given any jobs of any responsibility at that time because they-- they couldn't because of that attitude.
They would have to get over their attitude first.
That was a very sad situation, I thought.
Yes.
In 1945, I talked to my commanding officer.
I wanted to take a trip down there.
He said, you can't do that.
I have to make-- I have to give you a job of some sort to make it official.
So he gave me some sort of an official job.
And I didn't have to do anything, of course.
And I've got a Jeep and a driver and I got a-- I took a trip to Wiesbaden.
I-- just a one-day trip.
And I visited the city-- the house where we used to live, where it was occupied by American soldiers.
I went in there and I mentioned-- I said, I used to live here.
Could I take a look around?
Sure, make yourself at home.
[LAUGHS] And I did look around.
It looked very much-- pretty much the same.
A little bit dilapidated, but pretty much the same as it was.
And I visited two people.
I visited a colleague of my father who my father always said he was not a Nazi.
And I stopped by.
He was very uneasy when I visited him and very-- not very pleasant.
He answered my questions and he asked about my father, but that's about as far as it went.
And I noticed that he was very uneasy talking to me.
I couldn't figure out what it was.
I found out later I can come.
That is another story that I should have mentioned earlier.
His son-- is it all right to go back to that?
Sure.
His son was a very good friend of mine in the gymnasium.
He never did anything anti-Semitic or took part in any actions with others where something took place.
He was always as pleasant and as nice to me as possible.
I went to his house, he came to my house.
He taught me how to play chess, in fact.
I Remember one thing about it.
We got along very well.
And then one day, I noticed I hadn't seen him for a while.
And I called him up and he-- and he answered.
I said-- I said, yeah, I've been busy.
I haven't been able to come and I'll come one of these days.
And I didn't think anything of it.
And a few days-- a week or so later, I took my bike, I went up to his house as I did many times.
And his parents were on the balcony there.
And they said, you can't come in.
They said, what are you doing here anyway?
I said, oh, I wanted to see Ralph.
They said, you can't see him.
I said, why-- why not?
They said, go home.
They said, we don't want you to come here anymore.
I said, did I do something?
They said, no, you didn't do anything.
I thought, you know, I must have done something wrong and-- and I couldn't understand it.
And I went back home again and I told my father about it.
And he said, hm, they must have gotten to him.
And I found out later that he was threatened.
They said, you your son is playing with a Jewish boy.
If you know what's good for him, see to it that he doesn't do that anymore.
And later I was invited by the City of Wiesbaden, by the way, two years ago.
That is-- I was asked whom I would like to see in Wiesbaden.
And Ralph was the one person I wanted to see.
I never had anything against him.
And I found out that he had-- did not want to see me.
I tried a few times to try to get somebody-- the-- somebody who taught at the school and who was active in the city and who knew him, actually, to talk to him.
But he said, no, he doesn't want to see me.
And I was told also that his father was quite a-- a Nazi.
But my father always said, no, he's not a Nazi.
And he always was on very good terms with him and I was on very good terms with his son.
So sometimes you don't know people.
I came back in January, 1946.
I went back home again.
I went back to the firm where I worked before.
And business was very slow.
A lot of the diamond-cutting businesses went out-- went bankrupt.
And they stayed in business for I think a month or two and then they closed shop because the diamond industry was dead as far as New York is concerned.
It went to Palestine, to Puerto Rico for the less expensive stones.

And there was some-- there was also a clique of-- of diamond dealers and cutters from Antwerp who stuck very closely together and got sort of-- got rid of all the-- the people who joined later who did not belong to that group.
And I think to this date, the diamond industry is still run by that-- that clique.
And then I joined the ranks of the unemployed for a while.
And I-- while I was in the service, I liberated a camera.
[LAUGHS] You know, when you first moved into Neustadt, all of the local population had to give up all binoculars and cameras.
Of course, the officers had first pick of the cameras.
They got all the good ones and were-- we were left with the little box cameras and the cheapies.
But there was one camera that I remembered, which was a roller cord, which looked like a box camera and the officers didn't want.
So I took that one.
And I got the local photographer in town to teach me how to use it and he developed the films and he gave me some pointers.
And as a child, I was always-- I once-- I did my own developing and everything.
As a child, already I had a-- sort of a box camera, took pictures.
And-- and once there was an ad in the somebody was wanted-- an amateur photographer wanted who had floodlights, a tripod, and-- and a reflex camera, and a car.
The only thing I had is a-- a reflex camera.
[SIGHS] That might be something for Elaine to-- [LAUGHS] of interest.
And so I answered that ad and it was in Brooklyn.
They interviewed me and they said, yes, we can use any one who knows how to use that camera but you have to have a car and you have to have lights.
But we can help you out.
We can get the lights for you, we can get a tripod for you.
We'll take it out of here first couple of weeks salary, whatever you earn.
But you have to have a car.
And I always wanted a car and my parents never had a car.
And I said, this is my excuse to get a car.
I had a little bit of money saved from before and my mother saved all the money that I sent back from the army, my-- she bought bonds from that and so on.
Well, I didn't use that money.
That became my dowry later on.

But I had enough money to buy, I think was $100 for a-- $300 for a car that was an ancient jalopy, which lasted about three weeks and I just couldn't use it for work and I got rid of it, got myself a little better car.
But I found out-- I learned how to take baby pictures.
And what happened was they said, oh you don't have to-- I didn't know what the front or back of a baby was.
And they said, we'll train you.
So they sent me out with a guy who had been working there for two weeks before.
And he took me to one or two sittings.
He said, well, you put up a table; you put the baby on there; you put one light here; you [? put ?] one light there; you put the background up; and you try to get the baby to smile.
[SIGHS]
So we went to one or two sittings and he said, well, I've got to go.
I have an appointment someplace.
They'll send you out with somebody else in the morning.
I went back in the morning.
They said, well, we have so much work to do, you'll have to do the best you can.
I said, I don't know how to take baby pictures.
Well, do the best you can.
And I found out it was a racket, actually, that they sold coupons to people for a-- for a picture and try to sell everything but the kitchen sink after that.
And they did horrible work.
And I didn't know that.
Of course, I was very naive.
But I learned how to take baby pictures.
I went, took the picture, did the best I could.
Of course, the first day I went out, every baby-- baby-- baby was screaming his head off and I couldn't get a single smile out of a baby.
And I came back and expected to be fired.
They ?] said, yeah, you're doing all right.
Try to get a couple of more smiles out of the babies and keep that backdrop back a little bit further.
There's too much of a shadow on it.
So I-- I worked there for about a week or so and then I went to the darkroom and said, my gosh, I can do better work than that with my eyes closed.
And they sent the pictures out COD, the most horrible work they knocked out.
And I couldn't take it anymore.
I stopped.
I quit.
But then I noticed there was a-- an ad there for an experienced photographer.
And of course, after two weeks, I was experienced.
I knew where-- what the front-- which way the front, which way the back of the baby was and I knew how to get a baby to smile.
And I worked for that firm and they were happy with my work.
And I worked there for two or three months.
And they happened to go out of business, like a lot of baby business.
And then I went through a procession of studios that came and went.
There was a baby boom that started with lots of babies, lots of competition, lots of fly by night studios.
And then I figured I knew enough about it to go out on my own.
I was on my own for a while with very moderate success because I-- I couldn't do enough-- I couldn't make a good living on it.
So I figured I'd join an established studio.
And then I was with one studio for many, many years and then another studio for over 35 years and-- until my retirement.
And I did fairly well.
I made a living, good living.
What year did you married your wife?
We married in 1950.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
--we met accidentally on-- well, a friend of mine belonged to a youth group of a temple.
I was never much of a joiner of anything.
And he said, you know, we're going away for a weekend and would you like to come along?
I said, oh, I don't know.
I'm not into temples and-- oh, there's some nice, good-looking girls there.
So my ears perked up a little bit.
I said, what are you going to do there?
Oh, we're going to have fun.
It's-- just going to go away.
It's a summer-- it's a winter resort in Allenville and we're meeting at the Hotel Dixie and we're gonna take a train or bus up there.
So I said, I don't know.
He said, do me a favor, come along.
You'll-- I'm sure you'll like it.
He twisted my arm.
I said, all right.
OK, I'll go.
And we met at the Hotel Dixie.
It was the meeting place.
There's also a bus terminal.
And there was a snowstorm coming up and all the buses were canceled.
So we went to Pennsylvania Station and took a train to Allenville.
Somebody made arrangements there to-- for us to be picked up.
And at the station, I met Teki and we started talking.
And I think it was love at first sight.
And we took to each other and after that, I tried to play-- I wanted to call her.
I didn't want to appear too anxious so I waited a week or two.
And she talked to my friend and said, gee, we got along so fine.
I'm surprised Warren hasn't called me.
And then I finally called her.
And well, it's-- we've been together ever since.
And when you interview her, you're gonna get an earful of her story, too.
[LAUGHS]
Oh, yes.
My mother, actually, had-- it was my mother's cousin.
And she disappeared like so many.
My grandparents, of course, also were sent to a concentration camp.
I didn't mention that.
They moved into our-- our house in Wiesban, actually, when we left to go overseas.
And in 1943, I believe, they were-- all-- all the Jews in Wiesbaden were deported to Theresienstadt.
And very, very few of them returned.
And my grandparents and that-- my mother's cousin's mother also was deported.
But she somehow disappeared.
We never heard from her again.
We knew-- we didn't know what had happened to her.
And after-- years after the war, somehow she got in touch with I don't know whether it was my mother or somebody.
She had-- she-- they called it [GERMAN], they submerged.
And she was-- she was hidden somewhere with non-Jews and survived the war.
And later on, she settled in East Berlin.
And she-- already before that, she was not a youngster anymore.
She was a young lady before the war and she studied opera and became a singer.
And after the war, she became a singer at the German State Opera in East Berlin.
She was not a top singer, but she always sang, from what my mother told me, supporting roles.
And my mother tried very hard to get her to come to-- here.
She thought she would do very well here.
But no.
First of all she had a husband there and also later on a family.
And she was a devout communist, had become a devout communist, and would not leave Eastern Berlin.
Her name was Adelhaid Muller Hess.
It's a double name.
Hess is her maiden name and Muller is her married name.
And she did meet with my mother in Switzerland once.
And I have some pictures of her children also.

My uncle was in touch with her for a while, but somehow we lost contact and I don't even know what-- it might be interesting to get in touch with her children, but I have no idea what their names are or whether they would have any interest.
Well, I hope I haven't rambled on too much with this.
I realize that as a survivor, quote unquote, it's a very-- I sometimes don't feel-- I feel guilty calling myself a survivor.
I just happened to be there and happened to be at the right place at the right time and haven't been really responsible for my own survival.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In the beginning, I mentioned that when we first came to this country, I also had to do what I could to survive in a way.
And I had all kinds of jobs.
After school I worked at a dry cleaners delivering clothes, picking up clothes, delivering.
Before school, I delivered newspapers and during vacation or on weekends very often, I shined shoes.
But in Washington Heights, it wasn't such an affluent area.

Not too many people could afford to have their shoes shined, so I had to travel quite a bit with a chair and a box to an area and usually settled on midtown Manhattan, which was slightly more affluent.
It only cost a nickel to go to-- by subway to go there.
But by the same token, a shoeshine only was a nickel on weekdays.
On weekends, it was a dime.

And there were times when I didn't have any customers and I didn't have a nickel to go back with the subway so I had to go from 86th Street to 153rd Street carrying a chair and my shoeshine box and walking home without having done anything.
And once I remember my mother visited me.
And it was a terribly hot day and I was sitting on the sunny side of the street where the sun hit the-- she said, why don't you go over to the shady area and sit there and wait for customers?
I said, no.
Nobody has their shoes shined on that area.
That's not a busy area.
So she went into a store, she bought me a Coke.
And she spent-- I chided her, you spent a nickel on a Coke for me and I didn't have a single customer yet today.
And I don't even have the car fare-- made the car fare to go home.
You should have saved that nickel.
So everything-- every nickel counted.
When we bought-- hm?
I need you to stop.
We're at the end.
